items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What do orthodox Christians believe about the nature of Jesus?
items.correct: He is fully God and fully human at the same time (the incarnation) — both natures united in one person, as defined at the Council of Chalcedon.
items.misconception: Jesus was half God and half human.
items.random_wrong: Jesus was a human who became God after his resurrection.
items.why: The 'half and half' model was historically rejected as heresy. Orthodox doctrine holds both natures fully and simultaneously — a deliberately paradoxical claim students often soften.

items.q: Is the doctrine of the Trinity stated explicitly in the New Testament?
items.correct: Not as a formula — the NT contains triadic patterns (e.g. Matthew 28:19), but the developed doctrine (one ousia, three hypostases) was articulated through the councils of the fourth century; the word 'Trinity' never appears in scripture.
items.misconception: The New Testament explicitly states the full doctrine of one substance and three persons.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity was invented in the Middle Ages with no biblical roots.
items.why: Doctrine develops from scriptural raw material: Nicaea (325) and Constantinople (381) forged the technical vocabulary. Both 'it's all on the page' and 'it's a late invention' miss how doctrine forms.

items.q: Did the Council of Chalcedon explain how Christ's two natures are united?
items.correct: No — Chalcedon set boundaries: one person in two natures 'without confusion, change, division, separation', deliberately ruling out errors without offering a positive mechanism.
items.misconception: Chalcedon provided a complete scientific explanation of how divinity and humanity combine in Christ.
items.random_wrong: Chalcedon decided that Christ had only a divine nature.
items.why: The four negative adverbs fence the mystery rather than dissolve it — excluding Eutyches and Nestorius alike. Students who expect an explanatory theory misread what conciliar definitions do.

items.q: Which Greek term affirms that the Son shares the Father's substance?
items.why: It's Homoousios. “Homoiousios” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Chalcedon (451) is best known for defining which doctrine?
items.why: Correct answer: The hypostatic union. “The procession of the Spirit” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the First Council of Nicaea take place?
items.why: Exact answer: 325. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
